cards.tip: FIX — on a leaking, dangerously-low 2006 system 13 years past service with a second failing unit, build tiered repair-vs-replace options on both the house and casita and tee up a comfort-advisor visit before leaving.
FIX ??? on a 13-year-old system with original owners, come back from the van with built options and a clear recommendation instead of accepting "just note it and we'll see."

FIX ΓÇö when he threatened to call another company you folded with 'whatever you want,' so next time acknowledge the frustration and lock in a firm return date so we don't hand the maintenance to a competitor.

FIX ΓÇö the customer asked outright for duct cleaning and floated air-quality upgrades, so nail that interest down into a firm quote you book today instead of deferring it to a maybe-later pricing check.
WIN — you didn't leave empty-handed: you captured the four $90 filters on the spot and rebooked the maintenance around the nap window, so keep that recover-the-trip instinct on every visit.
FIX — before heading to the outside unit, pin down the system's age, repair history, and the customer's real goal so you come back inside ready to present strong repair-or-replace options.
FIX ΓÇö during the walkthrough, ask what comfort problems they've actually noticed so you uncover a reason to build options instead of just swapping the filter.
FIX ΓÇö Great intro; now bring that same energy to the on-mic report review and turn at least one inspection finding into a clear next-step option so a clean tune-up flips into a lead.

WIN â€” you flipped this by nerding out on the fan-motor oil leak and the 125-vs-113 amp draw to make an invisible problem feel urgent; next time convert that trust into two priced options on the spot instead of handing it to a booked estimate.

FIX â€” the second a homeowner says 'might as well buy brand new,' stop selling the cap and lock the free comfort-advisor appointment on the spot instead of punting to 'that's a different department.'
FIX â€” turn that solid diagnosis into a clear good/better/best option set and reconnect to their 'keep it running a few more years' goal instead of leaving them on a thaw-and-wait.

WIN â€” you nerded out the whole system so well they let you call the comfort advisor to the door on the spot; keep teeing up that replacement estimate mid-visit, and next time cap it with the 5-star review ask.

WIN â€” you turned an 'insurance will handle it' stall into a booked estimate by nerding out on the whole system and framing our replacement quote as leverage for their home warranty, so run that warranty-leverage play on every home-shield customer (and tack on the 5-star review ask you skipped).
FIX â€” after the outside inspection, reconnect face-to-face with photos and build at least one option instead of letting it end at 'just email me the report.'

WIN â€” you turned an aging two-system inspection into a free repair-vs-replacement comparison lead by framing Option C as no-charge, no-obligation 'see where you stand'; run that same play every time immediate repairs stack past $3,000, but pin a firm callback date instead of a vague follow-up.
FIX â€” on an aging original system, cut the small talk and ask real comfort and system-age discovery questions so a $0 maintenance becomes a repair or replacement opportunity.
FIX â€” get into targeted comfort discovery early instead of warranty small talk, so you surface issues you can build options around before wrapping the tune-up.
WIN â€” you translated the compressor amp readings straight into 'higher energy bills,' and that made the value land enough to earn a request for written estimates; do that tie-in on every diagnosis.

WIN â€” Your genuine rapport and honest free-fix diagnosis earned so much trust the customer asked for the membership themselves; keep that honesty and next time stack a good/better/best so a $16 membership grows into a real ticket.

FIX â€” when the customer volunteered the system was old and 'wearing and tearing,' empathize and ask a question to build an option or set a Comfort Advisor visit instead of punting the numbers to the emailed report.
FIX â€” your expectations setup at the door was textbook; now make sure every concern you find like that downstairs leak gets turned into clear written options and a review ask before you leave.
FIX â€” You found water stains in two rooms and an aging thermostat, so convert findings like that into a documented recommendation or a lead instead of letting the visit stay all rapport.

FIX â€” you nailed the system teaching but never put a number on the table, so start the homeowner approval call in parallel and leave a written temp-charge-vs-R22-replacement option so there's something concrete to close.

WIN â€” your 'be a nerd' teardown on why sinking $9,378 into a 1998 R22 system is throwing good money after bad is what flipped them to the replacement estimate; keep leading with that logic, and next time lock the comfort-advisor appointment before you leave.

WIN â€” you flipped a $39 tune-up into the $16/month membership by doing the annual-cost math against the $199 one-time clean and stacking value (2 visits, plumber flush, discounts), so keep leading with that math and lock the card on file before you walk outside.
FIX â€” when the owner isn't home, snap photos of what you find and get her on the phone with options before you leave so the maintenance can flip into an approved repair.
FIX â€” after a clean in-spec diagnosis, don't stop at 'you're good'; reconnect on their original worry and build 2-3 options so there's a next step to say yes to.
FIX â€” the customer asked new-unit pricing twice and said he plans to replace this winter, so book the free comfort-advisor estimate right there instead of deflecting with 'I'm just the fix-it guy.'

FIX â€” finish the full diagnosis, then come back inside and lay out clear built repair-vs-replace options with the numbers ($600 contactor / ~$6,000 compressor) instead of leaving the customer waiting.
FIX â€” after the equipment walkthrough, ask 2-3 discovery questions and lay out clear repair options so the strong diagnosis turns into a recommendation instead of ending on an explanation.

FIX â€” Before leaving, turn the diagnosis into a specific priced repair option and lock a firm return appointment instead of a vague 'come back later,' so the customer commits to the fix on the spot.
Pull the account's install and service history before you knock so you're not learning it live from the homeowner â€” it undercuts your expert cred right after an otherwise strong opening.

WIN â€” your plain-language teardown of the frozen coil and compressor amp-draw earned the customer's trust, then you flipped it by getting the comfort advisor on-site immediately; keep pairing that clear diagnosis with an instant handoff.

FIX â€” the customer was anxious and ready to move today, so nail down the motor availability, price, and timeline and put one clear repair option in front of him instead of staying in open-ended diagnosis.
Build the 5-star review ask into your close even on repair-scheduling calls like this one, not just same-day sales -- everything else in this visit was complete and well-delivered.
As soon as you finish diagnosing both problem units, turn straight into presenting options on the spot instead of letting the momentum drop after the bad news.
Take 30 seconds up front to lay out the visit plan and the approval-based work expectation before starting -- it's missing here even though the technical rapport was good.
Before dispatching the part for the soft-start swap, walk whoever is authorizing it through the cost, not just the diagnosis, so there's no surprise when the invoice shows up in email.
Keep the report-and-price close on the recording -- the diagnosis and options here were handled great, just make sure the final estimate and review ask actually get captured before wrapping up.
Make sure the recorder stays live through the whole visit -- your welcome here was excellent, but the assessment and options conversation isn't on this tape at all so it can't be coached.
Keep the recorder active through the full visit -- it cuts out right as the inspection starts, so the diagnostic and options conversation where the real coaching value is isn't captured.

Before leaving, lock in a concrete next step -- a scheduled comfort-advisor call or a specific follow-up day -- instead of ending on 'go from here,' and close the loop with the 5-star review ask you promised at the start.
Take 30 seconds to lay out the game plan and the approval-based expectation before heading out to start, even when the customer is doing most of the talking during intake.

When the customer calls you a lifesaver, that's your cue â€” ask for the 5-star review right there, and treat 'I'll just wait' as an invitation to ask one more question and book the free equipment-specialist visit instead of crossing it off.
When the decision-maker isn't home, get them on the phone before you leave and lock in the comfort advisor appointment â€” never end a dead-system call with 'she'll call us back.'

When the customer says the price is too much, don't pack up â€” give one empathy line and ask one question, then offer a second option, because a customer who asked you twice to find the leak is telling you they want it fixed.

Great same-day flip â€” now finish the call the same way you start it: recap what you found in one sentence and ask for the 5-star review by name before you walk out, because that customer was ready to give it and you never asked.
You seed the 5-star experience at the welcome on every call â€” now close the loop at the wrap-up and actually ask for the review before you walk out the door.

Great rapport and evidence, but stop quoting a repair price in the yard â€” hold every number until you're seated with good-better-best on paper, so the customer weighs your options instead of taking your $600 anchor to the home warranty.

When a customer tells you the replacement is happening this week no matter what, re-offer the same-day comfort advisor turnover tied to their deadline instead of settling for tomorrow â€” and never leave without asking for the 5-star review.
